title: Episode 4

jokes.text: Fleabag to Claire: 'Just don't tell her you got the statue from me, OK?'
jokes.setup: Context: Fleabag stole a sculpture and is now gifting it — the casualness of this admission
jokes.punchline: The single practical concern she has is just 'don't mention my name' — no guilt, no remorse, only logistics
jokes.explanation: Fleabag's moral confidence — treating theft as a minor logistical problem — is very much her character, and the line is economical. It doesn't land as big on first watch because it's expository, but it's efficiently funny.

jokes.text: 'You're just tipping your prime.'
jokes.setup: Brief context from the opening montage — appears to be someone offering a compliment or dismissal
jokes.punchline: The phrasing 'tipping your prime' is a deliciously odd construction — implies the prime is a cup about to spill
jokes.explanation: Without more context it's hard to place this fully, but the language is pleasingly strange. 'Tipping your prime' is an unexpected phrase — it works as a mildly funny bit of word choice, if not a full joke.

jokes.text: Fleabag can't admit she's gripping the door handle during Claire's driving — 'Let go of the handle, then.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag insists she's not judging Claire's driving
jokes.punchline: Claire calls her bluff by telling her to let go of the panic handle — implying Fleabag's body is physically betraying her denial
jokes.explanation: Smart, efficient sisterly banter — the detail of the handle is the kind of hyper-specific, true-to-life beat that Fleabag excels at. The physicality implied here (Fleabag white-knuckling the handle) does real comedic work. Excellent economy: three words, one devastating point.

jokes.text: Fleabag orders a vibrator for 'my very sexually frustrated sister'
jokes.setup: A clerk asks 'For you?' about a product order — the normal follow-up
jokes.punchline: Fleabag answers with far too much information about her sister's sex life, completely unbothered
jokes.explanation: Pure Fleabag — she overshares with cheerful bluntness. The joke is in who she is: utterly unbothered by social norms. It's character-specific and efficient, and it plants the sister dynamic plus foreshadows the vibrator payoff later. The rewatch bonus applies because this gag has a full payoff in the tiny-batteries sequence.

jokes.text: The sexual harassment case euphemism exchange — 'We haven't had the opportunity to support any women-led businesses since the... Sexual harassment case.' 'Yes, the sexual harassment case.'
jokes.setup: Someone starts a diplomatic euphemism, trailing off as if the word is too delicate to say
jokes.punchline: The other person just says it plainly — 'Sexual harassment case' — and both parties then repeat it straight, as if now both comfortable with the word
jokes.explanation: A neat, efficient piece of workplace irony — the absurdity that 'sexual harassment case' is itself the embarrassing thing that must be danced around, then immediately said anyway. The structure is clean but it's a quick background gag, not deeply character-specific.

jokes.text: 'Do you know what the lesbian app for Grindr is called? Twat Nav.'
jokes.setup: Sisters have just argued about the sat-nav; Fleabag has a sudden gear-shift into a joke
jokes.punchline: 'Twat Nav' — a portmanteau of 'twat' (British vulgar slang for female genitalia) and 'Sat Nav' — lands as both wordplay and a subject-change gag
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best pure joke — a perfectly constructed two-beat setup/punchline that earns its punchline through the earlier sat-nav argument. 'Twat Nav' is an original, quotable coinage with real wit: it's a pun, a cultural reference, and a joke about lesbian identity, all in two words. The setup ('Do you know what the lesbian app for Grindr is called?') mimics the rhythm of a classic stand-up structure, and it lands in the middle of an emotionally loaded scene, which makes it funnier and somehow more poignant. It is exactly the kind of joke Fleabag uses to deflect. Genuinely exceptional economy and originality — comparable in quality to the 8.0 anchor, perhaps better.

jokes.text: 'Don't make this fun!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just made Claire laugh with the 'Twat Nav' joke, mid-emotional crisis
jokes.punchline: Claire's protest — 'Don't make this fun!' — is itself the funniest possible response: it reveals how desperately Claire wants to stay miserable and closed off
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly encapsulate the Claire-Fleabag relationship — Claire is furious that she's been made to laugh when she's committed to her emotional fortification. 'Don't make this fun!' is an absurd sentence that only makes sense coming from this repressed character. It's high character-integration and brilliant economy. The joke is the character. This rivals 'Twat Nav' for the episode's best moment, and the two are stronger together.

jokes.text: Claire immediately sobs after laughing
jokes.setup: The laughter beat from 'Twat Nav' / 'Don't make this fun!'
jokes.punchline: Claire instantly dissolves into weeping — the tonal whiplash is the joke, and it's also the pivot into genuine pathos
jokes.explanation: The laugh-to-sob is a Fleabag signature move — comedy and grief occupying the same breath. Here it's earned, not cheap. The stage direction 'SHE SOBS' immediately following 'Don't make this fun!' creates the sharpest possible tonal cut. It IS the joke and the drama simultaneously.

jokes.text: 'It's OK, I'm fucking OK, I'm excellent.'
jokes.setup: Claire has just been sobbing
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'OK' to 'fucking OK' to 'I'm excellent' — each step more insistent and more clearly untrue
jokes.explanation: The structure escalates perfectly: each word adds more denial until 'excellent' becomes absurd. This is textbook character comedy for Claire — her entire being is about insisting she's fine. 'I'm excellent' while actively sobbing is a beautiful piece of writing. High character-integration score because this could only be Claire.

jokes.text: Claire: 'I just sometimes need you not to...' Fleabag finishes: 'To take the piss.' Claire: 'Don't finish my sentences!' — then they both fumble trying to finish the sentence differently
jokes.setup: Claire asks Fleabag to stop something; Fleabag finishes her sentence; Claire tells her not to do that
jokes.punchline: Both women then get tied in knots trying NOT to finish each other's sentences simultaneously, each trying to assert independence, making it worse
jokes.explanation: The joke has two phases: first Fleabag correctly finishes Claire's sentence (proof she does know), then both women's attempt to avoid that leads to comedic confusion. This is well-constructed escalation with strong character logic — their relationship's intimacy and irritability are both the setup and the punchline.

jokes.text: 'Fucking psycho.' (after a long, loaded silence in the car)
jokes.setup: An extended silence following the emotional conversation about Martin
jokes.punchline: Fleabag punctuates the silence with 'Fucking psycho' — presumably directed at herself, or at Martin, or at the situation; the ambiguity is part of the joke
jokes.explanation: Two words that break a tension-laden silence. Its target (Claire? Martin? herself?) is ambiguous, which makes it funnier and more true — Fleabag uses profanity as emotional punctuation. The economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: 'Wow! Dad really splashed out this time. He must be about to do something awful.' 'No, it's just Mother's Day.' 'Oh.'
jokes.setup: The sisters arrive and are impressed by an apparently generous gift
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's instinct is that generosity = guilt/manipulation from Dad; the correction 'it's just Mother's Day' deflates the conspiracy theory, and Fleabag's flat 'Oh' registers genuine surprise that it might just be... nice
jokes.explanation: Beautifully character-specific — Fleabag's worldview is that kindness has a price. The logic 'splashed out = about to do something awful' tells us so much about the family dynamic in one beat. The 'Oh' at the end is perfect deadpan understatement. Economy is excellent.

jokes.text: 'We're not supposed to bond on this, are we? Because I really don't think that's going to end well. We're not supposed to talk at all. It's a silent retreat, God help us.'
jokes.setup: The sisters have just arrived at what turns out to be a silent retreat
jokes.punchline: 'God help us' as a genuine prayer from a non-believer who needs help not talking
jokes.explanation: Solid comic framing of the premise — Fleabag immediately identifies that a silent retreat is the worst possible venue for two sisters who argue constantly. 'God help us' is a nice button. Functional rather than exceptional.

jokes.text: 'They're probably going to think we're a couple.' 'The fact that your mind even goes there is beyond disturbing.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag makes an observation about how they'll look arriving together at a retreat
jokes.punchline: Claire's horror is disproportionate and funny — the 'beyond disturbing' is wildly excessive for a mild joke about optics
jokes.explanation: Claire's overreaction is the joke — her rigidity makes her appalled by even harmless absurdity. Competent character comedy; not exceptional.

jokes.text: Fleabag, cheerfully: 'We'd make a really cute couple.'
jokes.setup: Claire has just said the idea is 'beyond disturbing'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag doubles down and compliments their hypothetical couplehood with genuine enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: The escalation from Fleabag — refusing to back down and going one further — is very much her character. The joke is that she's right (they probably would), and she enjoys Claire's discomfort.

jokes.text: The men's retreat next door shouts 'Sluts!' just as the sisters arrive at the women's retreat door
jokes.setup: The women are waiting politely at the door of a mindfulness retreat
jokes.punchline: An aggressive male voice yells 'Sluts!' from nearby — completely incongruous, immediately unsettling
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of a serene mindfulness setting with 'Sluts!' being screamed is a beautifully absurdist premise drop. It becomes a running gag that structures the episode. First instance establishes the conceit; later instances pay it off. The economy is perfect — one word, maximum dissonance.

jokes.text: Fleabag: 'I'm going to die here. We're going to be raped and die.' / 'Every cloud...'
jokes.setup: Still waiting at the door, after the 'Sluts!' shout, mild panic
jokes.punchline: 'Every cloud...' — implying there might be an upside to being raped and dying; the phrase is spectacularly inappropriate here and that's the joke
jokes.explanation: Fleabag's dark nihilism deployed in two words. 'Every cloud...' is perhaps the episode's sharpest example of comic understatement — an idiom applied to an atrocity. Very Fleabag, economical, and genuinely funny.

jokes.text: Host asks 'Two single beds or a double?' — Fleabag and Claire answer simultaneously and in opposite: Claire 'Two singles' / Fleabag 'A double, please.'
jokes.setup: A standard hospitality question
jokes.punchline: Fleabag requesting a double bed to share with her sister — precisely what Claire just rejected — shows Fleabag's commitment to winding Claire up
jokes.explanation: A tiny moment of brilliant sibling dynamics — Claire asserting appropriate boundaries, Fleabag immediately overriding them. It extends the 'couple' joke and reinforces the power dynamic between sisters. The simultaneous contradiction is perfectly timed.

jokes.text: 'What happened yesterday?' 'Sting wore white jeans and a puppy got stuck in a fan. Big day.'
jokes.setup: Claire challenges Fleabag's claim that she reads the news
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's 'news' is celebrity trivia and animal accidents — she does follow the news, it's just not real news. 'Big day' deadpans it into perfect absurdism
jokes.explanation: This is excellent comedy writing. The specificity ('Sting wore white jeans' — a real, vivid detail, and 'a puppy got stuck in a fan' — absurd but weirdly plausible tabloid-brain fodder) is what makes it. 'Big day' as a button is perfectly dry. It's character-specific (Fleabag absolutely would follow this version of news), original in its construction, and highly quotable. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor reference.

jokes.text: The full unpacking sequence: Fleabag catalogues Claire's creams by body part — neck/chest, legs/knees, ends of hair, under-eyes, face and body — getting increasingly specific and absurd
jokes.setup: Fleabag asks what each cream is for; Claire answers each straight
jokes.punchline: The sheer NUMBER of creams, their hyper-specificity (ends of hair! under-eyes!), and the escalating absurdity of each answer — this is a slow build with the list itself as the joke
jokes.explanation: A masterclass in list escalation. Each cream is more specific than the last, culminating in 'that is for my face and body' — which sounds enormous in contrast to 'ends of my hair.' The structure is deliberate and the character-work is perfect for Claire, who is the kind of person who absolutely has a cream for the ends of her hair.

jokes.text: 'What would you do if someone stole all of those?' 'I'd kill myself.'
jokes.setup: The climax of the cream list — Fleabag asks a hypothetical
jokes.punchline: Claire says she'd kill herself without hesitation or irony — delivered as a completely proportionate response
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's finest moments. The punchline to the cream list is Claire saying she would literally kill herself if the creams were stolen — and she means it as a fact, not hyperbole. The deadpan delivery makes it shocking and hilarious. It's perfectly structured as the payoff to an escalating list, it's maximally economical (five words), and it could only be Claire. The dark-subversive element works because Claire has clearly ordered her coping mechanisms around appearances. This earns the 8+ score comfortably.

jokes.text: 'Why are there no plug sockets in here?' — and the tiny batteries discovery
jokes.setup: Fleabag notices there are no plug sockets; Claire has small items in her bag labelled as 'nothing'; Fleabag innocently names their uses (remote controls, alarm clocks...)
jokes.punchline: 'And vibrators.' — 'Yes! All right!'
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the episode's opening joke. The slow build — remote controls, alarm clocks — follows perfect list-joke grammar before landing 'vibrators.' Claire's explosive 'Yes! All right!' is the correct response. It's a callback that earns real points: the earlier 'sexually frustrated' gag was setup; this is the punchline. High character-integration: Claire's denial then confirmation is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: 'If you want to have a wank, I can give you some space.' / 'I'll go into the bathroom and moisturise my wrists for a bit.'
jokes.setup: Claire is mortified that Fleabag has identified the vibrator
jokes.punchline: Fleabag offers to give Claire masturbation privacy — then immediately makes it weirder and more specific by describing exactly what she'll do in the bathroom (moisturise her WRISTS, specifically)
jokes.explanation: 'Moisturise my wrists' is a brilliantly specific absurdist detail — it's funny because it's so deliberate and so oddly pointed. It implies she has a whole ritual too, but for her wrists. The generosity of the offer is itself funny — Fleabag is being genuinely helpful by her own logic.

jokes.text: The motivational tape says 'Let go of your past' — Fleabag: 'Bit on the nose.'
jokes.setup: A motivational/mindfulness tape plays instructions
jokes.punchline: Fleabag fourth-wall-adjacent commentary — 'Bit on the nose' — acknowledging the obvious thematic relevance of 'let go of your past' to her entire life situation
jokes.explanation: Fleabag is essentially critiquing the show's own themes. 'Bit on the nose' is Fleabag's way of acknowledging her grief without engaging with it — comedic deflection as character work. The economy is superb. It's also meta-adjacent — the audience understands the full weight of 'let go of your past' in this story.

jokes.text: Fleabag realizes she's wandered into the MALE anti-misogyny workshop rather than the women's retreat
jokes.setup: We've been following Fleabag into what we assume is the women's retreat
jokes.punchline: The reveal that the 'Women don't speak' and all the male voices around her is because she's in the men's anti-misogyny workshop — she has to leave
jokes.explanation: The structural joke of the parallel retreat is brilliant — the female silent retreat runs alongside a men's misogyny rehabilitation workshop. Fleabag wandering into the wrong room is a perfect structural reveal. On rewatch, all the earlier 'Slut!' shouts snap into context and become even funnier.

jokes.text: 'Trapping your thoughts in your skull. Think of it as a thought prison in your mind.'
jokes.setup: The retreat leader is explaining the concept of mindfulness
jokes.punchline: 'Thought prison in your mind' — a bizarre, dystopian way to describe meditation that undermines the serenity the retreat is supposed to offer
jokes.explanation: The inadvertent menace of 'thought prison in your mind' delivered earnestly by a wellness professional is very funny. It's an absurdist phrasing that reveals the prison-like nature of enforced silence without the speaker being aware of it. Waller-Bridge is writing from inside the wellness-industry's language to expose its own darkness.

jokes.text: During meditation, a vibrator buzzes loudly from one of the bags — 'Shh!'
jokes.setup: The women are meditating in silence; Claire has brought a vibrator to the retreat
jokes.punchline: The vibrator turns on during meditation — the buzzing is unmistakable — and the leader shushes it
jokes.explanation: The third leg of the vibrator callback — it was established, it was revealed, now it fires (literally) at the worst possible moment. The leader shushing it as if it's a coughing person is peak Fleabag absurdism. Perfectly timed callback with physical comedy.

jokes.text: During meditation: 'Slut!' / 'Bitch!' / 'Bitch, bitch!' — shouts bleeding through from the men's workshop
jokes.setup: The women are in silent meditation; the men next door are apparently practicing not saying these words
jokes.punchline: The words erupt repeatedly into the women's silence — ironic, since the men are supposedly learning NOT to say them
jokes.explanation: The running gag is now fully operational and the irony is perfectly constructed: a women's silence retreat being interrupted by men who are being workshopped to stop calling women 'slut' and 'bitch' — by shouting 'slut' and 'bitch.' The escalation ('Bitch, bitch!') is lovely. Strong structural satire.

jokes.text: After the men struggle to say anything positive, one finally says 'Well done, Patricia.' — and the facilitator's response: 'Very good. Please leave.'
jokes.setup: The group has finally said 'Well done, Patricia' after an agonizing pause
jokes.punchline: 'Very good. Please leave.' — the facilitator dismisses Patricia immediately after praising the men, as if her presence was only ever instrumental
jokes.explanation: 'Please leave' — delivered immediately after her use is served — is itself a piece of casual dehumanization inside a lesson about not dehumanizing women, which is pointed social satire in four syllables.

jokes.text: Men's workshop — 'What should we not say when we meet Patricia?' 'Clever little munchkin?' / 'Who did you blow to get that job?' / 'Slut, you fucking stupid slut.'
jokes.setup: The workshop leader asks men what NOT to say to a woman who's been promoted
jokes.punchline: The men's answers escalate from condescending ('clever little munchkin') to sexually hostile to outright violent — none of them answer with 'nothing'; instead they each demonstrate exactly the problem
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly structured escalation. The point of the exercise is to NOT say these things, but the men eagerly recite a catalogue of misogyny, each worse than the last. 'Clever little munchkin' is almost charming in its obliviousness; 'slut, you fucking stupid slut' is pure id. The men understand the exercise perfectly — and still can't stop. This is dark satire doing real work.

jokes.text: 'What should we say to her?' [long pause] 'Well done, Patricia.' 'Very good.'
jokes.setup: After a long pause, one man finally produces the correct answer
jokes.punchline: It takes a room full of men several seconds of visible mental effort to arrive at 'well done' — and when they do, it's presented as an achievement deserving congratulations
jokes.explanation: The silence before 'Well done, Patricia' does enormous comedic work — it implies genuine cognitive struggle. Then 'very good' treating it as an achievement is pitch-perfect. The joke is that basic human decency requires a workshop and multiple attempts. Sharp satire with excellent structural execution.

jokes.text: The men then add 'Slut!' after saying 'Well done, Patricia' — they can't help themselves
jokes.setup: The group has just successfully said 'Well done, Patricia' — a tiny victory
jokes.punchline: One man immediately appends 'Slut!' — the veneer of progress is instantly demolished
jokes.explanation: A devastating button to the workshop scene. The one word added after 'Well done, Patricia' undoes everything. It's an economic, structurally perfect joke about how shallow behavioral change can be.

jokes.text: 'Literally her worst nightmare.' (Fleabag to camera, during the palm-touching meditation exercise)
jokes.setup: The retreat leader asks women to mirror their palms and touch each other; Claire is known to be closed-off and physically averse
jokes.punchline: Fleabag breaks the fourth wall to comment directly on Claire's horror at being asked to touch a stranger — 'Literally her worst nightmare'
jokes.explanation: A classic Fleabag direct-address moment. Three words, maximum efficiency. The camera turn is the joke's engine — she breaks the fourth wall to indict her sister to the audience, which is an act of intimacy with the viewer and further evidence of how well Fleabag knows Claire. The 'Eugh!' that follows confirms it.

jokes.text: Claire's visceral 'Eugh!' during the palm-touching exercise
jokes.setup: 'Literally her worst nightmare' — the prediction Fleabag just made
jokes.punchline: Claire's involuntary sound of revulsion at being touched — exactly as predicted — is the proof
jokes.explanation: The one-word confirmation of Fleabag's prediction. The joke is that Claire's body expresses what she never would — and the 'Eugh!' is a perfect character sound. The callback structure makes it hit harder.

jokes.text: 'I can't feel my feet.' (during the shared bed scene after the meditation)
jokes.setup: Fleabag and Claire are sharing a bed — extremely awkward given the earlier dynamic
jokes.punchline: Claire's only communication is a clinical physical complaint — 'I can't feel my feet' — which is both literal and a comic portrait of her emotional inaccessibility
jokes.explanation: A small but effective character beat — Claire's body expressing what she won't. Efficient; functions as both mild comedy and character development.

jokes.text: 'When we were ten and cute. Now we're 30 and angry.'
jokes.setup: Discussion about whether they used to sleep top-to-toe as children
jokes.punchline: Claire's summary: ten and cute vs. 30 and angry — delivered as pure fact
jokes.explanation: '30 and angry' is a quietly perfect line — it's Claire's unguarded moment of self-description. The parallel structure ('ten and cute / 30 and angry') is economical and genuinely insightful comedy. Quotable in the way that small truths are.

jokes.text: Fleabag steals Claire's vibrator and checks 'if it's working' — 'Oh, it's working, it's definitely working.' KNOCKING follows
jokes.setup: Claire told Fleabag not to touch her things; Fleabag immediately touches the vibrator
jokes.punchline: 'It's definitely working' — confirmed during an inspection that is clearly too long, followed by knocking from next door (other retreat participants hearing it)
jokes.explanation: Fourth leg of the vibrator gag — now Fleabag has actively stolen it and tested it, to the audible displeasure of neighbors. The knocking is perfect physical comedy inferred from context. 'Have fun.' is a nice button if it follows (see next joke).

jokes.text: 'Have fun.' (from the neighbor, through the wall, after the vibrator goes off and knocking fails to stop it)
jokes.setup: Knocking to complain about the vibrator noise has failed; the neighbor gives up
jokes.punchline: 'Have fun.' — resignation as punchline; the neighbor has accepted the situation
jokes.explanation: Two words as perfect button. 'Have fun' is a masterclass in economy — the neighbor has processed, judged, and moved on. The silent retreat context makes it funnier (they shouldn't be talking at all either).

jokes.text: 'It's actually a really thoughtful present. Thank you.' (Claire to Fleabag, in the dark, after a silence — about the vibrator)
jokes.setup: The mortifying vibrator sequence has just concluded
jokes.punchline: Claire, in the dark, sincerely thanks Fleabag for the thoughtful gift — which is the vibrator she was just embarrassed about
jokes.explanation: The emotional payoff to the vibrator arc. In the dark, having had her dignity shredded, Claire quietly says it was actually thoughtful — and she means it. The comedy and warmth collapse into each other perfectly. This is Fleabag's signature mode: a joke that is also an act of genuine tenderness. The character-integration score is near-perfect because this is ONLY Claire: pride yielding to gratitude in darkness, where nobody can see her face.

jokes.text: 'And Martin getting me that sculpture. He must have bent over backwards to get something like that.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag knows the sculpture was stolen; Claire doesn't know Fleabag knows; Claire is praising Martin's effort
jokes.punchline: 'Bent over backwards' — and the audience/Fleabag knows Martin merely accepted stolen goods. The dramatic irony makes Claire's sincere praise darkly funny
jokes.explanation: Solid dramatic irony — Claire praising Martin's generosity while Fleabag and the audience know the sculpture is stolen. The phrase 'bent over backwards' is mildly amusing in context but the joke's real strength is structural (what the audience knows vs. what Claire knows).

jokes.text: Fleabag confesses she stole the sculpture — 'and then I tried to sell it through Martin, but Martin took it and gave it to you'
jokes.setup: Claire has just been praising Martin's thoughtfulness
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's casual confession reveals that Martin didn't source the sculpture — he just pocketed it from a middleman deal. He's neither thoughtful nor a good fence
jokes.explanation: The confession is funny because of how it unfolds: the more Fleabag explains, the worse Martin looks — not intentionally malicious, but accidentally fencing stolen goods and presenting them as thoughtful gifts. Character-specific and well-structured.

jokes.text: Claire: 'I've always been insecure about my face.' / Fleabag: 'There's nothing wrong with your nose.'
jokes.setup: Claire says she's insecure about her face; Fleabag means to reassure her
jokes.punchline: 'There's nothing wrong with your nose' — Fleabag immediately singles out the specific feature, implying she's thought about it too, then when challenged doubles down: 'There's nothing wrong...'
jokes.explanation: A cringe-comedy masterpiece of well-intentioned incompetence. Fleabag's reassurance immediately identifies the specific insecurity ('your nose'), making it ten times worse. The escalation — Claire making her repeat it, Fleabag digging deeper — is perfectly structured. Very specific to the sibling dynamic: only someone who genuinely cares could wound this precisely.

jokes.text: Meditation prompt: 'If you could change anything in the whole world, what would it be?' Fleabag: 'My thighs.' Leader: 'In the whole world?' Fleabag: 'Don't tell anyone I said that.'
jokes.setup: Grand cosmic question posed in meditation: what would you change in the whole world?
jokes.punchline: 'My thighs.' — the grand opportunity met with completely personal vanity, followed by the embarrassed retraction
jokes.explanation: This is excellent. The joke works on multiple levels — it subverts the grandiosity of the question with immediate vanity, but 'Don't tell anyone I said that' reveals that Fleabag KNOWS the gap between her self-image and this answer. It's both a great joke and a character moment — the woman who presents as chaotic and self-aware admitting a vulnerability she's ashamed of. The economy is perfect. Justifies 7.9+ craft score.

jokes.text: 'We've paid them to let us clean their house in silence.'
jokes.setup: The women are doing menial tasks (cleaning) as part of the retreat's 'sanctuary in menial tasks'
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's summary of what they're actually doing — they paid for the privilege of providing free labor — is the joke and the critique simultaneously
jokes.explanation: A sharp, well-economized joke that doubles as cultural critique. The wellness industry charging women to clean is a real observation given genuine comic force here. Highly quotable, perfectly structured, efficient. The laughter that immediately follows from Claire is the best possible confirmation.

jokes.text: The retreat leader's alternatives for 'clients' — 'Clients.' / 'Inmates?' / 'Cleaners?' / 'Participants!'
jokes.setup: The leader refers to the women as 'students'; Fleabag or Claire corrects with 'clients'
jokes.punchline: The series of rejected synonyms escalates — 'Inmates?' is the funniest because it's the most accurate given the prison-like rules; the leader finally insists on 'Participants!' with exhausted authority
jokes.explanation: 'Inmates?' is the comedic highpoint — it's the callback to 'thought prison' and it's the most accurate description of their situation. The escalation from 'clients' to 'inmates' to 'cleaners' is elegantly constructed. The callback rewards attentive viewers.

jokes.text: 'I got the Finland promotion.' [extended emotional scene] 'Does that mean you're a millionaire now? Handy.'
jokes.setup: Claire reveals she's got a major promotion — this is serious, emotional news
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's first response is 'Does that mean you're a millionaire now? Handy.' — not wrong, but completely unexpected in register, and warmly Fleabag
jokes.explanation: 'Handy.' is the perfect one-word button — Fleabag's practical, un-sentimentalized take on her sister's success. The contrast between Claire's emotion and Fleabag's breezy financial observation is character-specific and funny. Reinforces that Fleabag deflects with humor even in genuinely important moments.

jokes.text: Jake 'freaks out if she's gone for longer than a day and he's got this thing about trying to get in the bath with her. He's 15.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag explains why Jake makes Claire's Finland promotion complicated
jokes.punchline: 'He's 15.' — two words that make the bath thing much worse; the delay before revealing his age is perfect comic timing
jokes.explanation: The construction is excellent: Fleabag describes the bath behavior first, lets it settle, then reveals 'He's 15.' — this is the correct joke grammar. If she'd said '15-year-old Jake tries to get in the bath with her' it would be less funny. The age reveal is the punchline. Dark-subversive (the Jake situation is genuinely disturbing) delivered with perfect economy. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor.

jokes.text: 'No, no. No, Jake. No!' (Fleabag miming/reacting to the Jake bath story)
jokes.setup: The revelation that Jake (15) tries to get in the bath with Claire
jokes.punchline: Fleabag's physical/verbal rejection of the mental image — 'No, no. No, Jake. No!' — as if she's telling off a dog
jokes.explanation: The escalating 'No's addressed to Jake (who isn't there) as if she can stop it happening are funny — the grammar of training a bad dog applied to a teenager's inappropriate behavior. The direct address to the absent Jake is a small bit of physical-verbal comedy.

jokes.text: 'Perfect place for your cold, cold heart.' / 'I know!'
jokes.setup: Fleabag is listing reasons why Finland would be perfect for Claire
jokes.punchline: 'Perfect place for your cold, cold heart' — Claire doesn't deny it, she concedes ('I know!') which makes it funnier and sadder
jokes.explanation: The joke's best element is Claire's 'I know!' — full agreement with the insult makes it warm rather than cutting. Claire accepting her cold-heartedness as fact is excellent character writing. The line itself is a classic Fleabag put-down with genuine affection underneath.

jokes.text: 'Your husband tried to kiss me on your birthday.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag has been arguing that Martin shouldn't hold Claire back; she invokes his opinion as a reason Claire should ignore him
jokes.punchline: The bombshell delivery — 'Your husband tried to kiss me on your birthday' — is timed as if it's just another argument point, not a revelation; the casual register is the darkly funny element
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most impactful comedy-drama moments. The line is funny because of HOW it's said — with the same casual rhythm as 'no more power suits' and 'fuckload of snow.' Fleabag drops a bombshell in the tone of someone mentioning the weather. The structure is brilliant: it arrives as a joke but lands as something that breaks both characters open. The economy (nine words, maximum devastation) is exceptional. This earns its 8+ score — the misdirection is expertly executed.

jokes.text: Claire's reaction: an extended silence after 'Your husband tried to kiss me on your birthday' — then doors opening and closing
jokes.setup: Fleabag has just revealed Martin kissed her
jokes.punchline: Claire's silence. Then she leaves. The silence IS the reaction — there is no verbal response that could be adequate
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most extraordinary comedic-dramatic moment. The silence is the joke and the grief simultaneously. Claire processes the betrayal in silence — entirely in character (she never shows pain publicly) — and then leaves. The comedy here is in the silence's length, its completeness, and the fact that the doors opening and closing are the only response. It's not a traditional joke but it is the episode's sharpest comedic execution: the absence of reaction as the fullest possible reaction. For Claire, who has insisted she is 'fine' and 'excellent' throughout, silence is the most revealing possible choice. The craft scores reflect exceptional structural and character integration — this is television writing at a high level.

jokes.text: 'Yeah, I...thought I recognised you.' / 'Fair enough.' / 'Probably for the best.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag encounters a man from one of the workshops — clearly there's a history or recognition between them
jokes.punchline: The exchange is masterfully elliptical — neither confirms what they recognize, 'Fair enough' and 'Probably for the best' are both absurd as conclusions to an unfinished interaction
jokes.explanation: An extraordinarily efficient piece of comedy — three lines that imply a whole history, sexual or otherwise, in the most understated possible way. 'Probably for the best' is a line that contains a complete story in four words. The comedy is in the gap between what's said and what's clearly meant.

jokes.text: 'I touched a colleague's breast. More than once. At a party.'
jokes.setup: The man has been revealed to be from the anti-misogyny workshop; he begins to explain himself to Fleabag
jokes.punchline: The escalating self-revelation: 'breast' is bad, 'more than once' is worse, 'at a party' adds the social context that makes it pathetically worse — each clause digs deeper
jokes.explanation: The grammar of self-incrimination — each additional phrase makes it worse — is very well-constructed. 'More than once' is the turn; 'at a party' is the embarrassing specificity that makes it feel pathetically, horribly real. His delivery of it as a confession rather than a defense is what makes the joke work.

jokes.text: 'I'm just a very...disappointing man.'
jokes.setup: The man has just confessed to workplace sexual harassment and being sent to a workshop
jokes.punchline: 'Disappointing' is a magnificently understated word for what he's described — it's almost too gentle, and his owning of it with such precision is both funny and strangely moving
jokes.explanation: A genuinely exceptional piece of writing. 'Disappointing' is the unexpected word — not 'bad' or 'awful' or 'terrible' — and its very mildness makes it more damning. It implies a man who had potential, who has let himself down, who is specific enough to know the exact register of his failure. The pause before 'disappointing' does real work. This is precisely the kind of line that becomes quotable beyond the show. The economy score is near-perfect. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor; the originality and character-integration push it above.

jokes.text: The Banker's monologue — what he actually wants — ending with 'I want to make her orgasm again. And again. Truly.' then 'I just want to cry. All the time.'
jokes.setup: He says the workshop keeps asking what he wants; he refuses to tell them; then he tells Fleabag
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from moving domesticity (cups from the dishwasher, watching his wife drink from them) into 'I want to make her orgasm again. And again. Truly.' — the sudden sexual content in an otherwise tender monologue is the comedy; then 'I just want to cry. All the time.' is the most human possible landing
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's most complex comedic moment — and arguably its emotional core. The monologue builds beautifully: small domestic desires (dishwasher cups, watching her drink from them) establish a tenderness, then 'I want to make her orgasm again. And again. Truly.' arrives with its 'Truly' — the one word that makes it both funny and devastating. 'Truly' is trying so hard to be taken seriously that it becomes the joke. Then 'I just want to cry. All the time' lands as the most authentic line in the scene. This is comedy that earns the emotion it creates. The structure is excellent, the originality is high (the cup detail is extraordinary), and it's fully character-integrated. This competes with 'Don't make this fun!' for the episode's best piece of writing.

jokes.text: The man's monologue about wanting to 'make my wife orgasm again. And again. Truly.' — delivered earnestly in a garden at a silent retreat
jokes.setup: The man is delivering an increasingly sincere, moving list of what he wants from life
jokes.punchline: The list escalates from 'take clean cups out of the dishwasher' to 'make her orgasm again. And again. Truly.' — the word 'Truly' doing impossible work
jokes.explanation: This is the other masterpiece of the episode. The entire monologue is extraordinary but the orgasm section is the crown jewel. The list moves from humble domestic desires (clean cups from the dishwasher, watching her drink from them in the morning) to 'make her orgasm again. And again.' — and then 'Truly.' The word 'Truly' is the greatest single-word comedy note in the episode. It's both ridiculous and completely sincere, cringe and moving. The escalation from cups to orgasm is perfectly calibrated. This is 8.6 craft and worth every point.

jokes.text: Boo's voicemail: 'Hi, this is Boo. I can't come to the phone right now, but please leave me a messiagio, and I'll get back to you.'
jokes.setup: Fleabag calls Boo — we've been shown Boo is dead; this is her voicemail, still active
jokes.punchline: 'Messiagio' — Boo's invented word for 'message' is a small, personal quirk that lands as both funny and heartbreaking; followed by 'Someone should probably disconnect that.'
jokes.explanation: The episode's most emotionally devastating joke. 'Messiagio' is Boo's private neologism — the kind of word that exists only between very close friends. Hearing it on a dead woman's voicemail while Fleabag stands alone in the woods is the show at its absolute best: a joke that is also a grief object. 'Someone should probably disconnect that' is Fleabag managing the unbearable through understatement — and it's both the correct and most heartbreaking possible response. This is Fleabag's entire mode in a single moment: comedy as survival in the face of grief. The economy, character-integration, and structure are near-perfect. This is the episode's defining moment and one of the finest things in the series. It earns a 9+ craft score.

tmdb_overview: Fleabag and her sister Claire reluctantly visit a female-only, silent retreat, courtesy of their father. Their enforced silence is interrupted, however, by an unusual neighbouring weekend workshop, explosive secrets coming to light, and a surprising connection with an old acquaintance.
